content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662          322-5660           322-5901          322-6441          322-6444

December 2, 1982


John M. Cohan
Deputy County Counsel
105 East Anapamu St.
Santa Barbara, CA  93101-2063

Re:  Advice Request No. A-82-197

Dear Mr. Cohan:

You have requested clarification of that portion of
Government Code Section 87100,* which states:

     No public official at any level of . . . local
government shall make [or] participate in the making
. . . [of] a governmental decision in which he knows or
has reason to know he has a financial interest.

     Emphasis added.

Specifically, you want to know how Section 87100 applies to
Section 87103(c), which provides that:

     An official has a financial interest in a decision
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably

_____________________
*All statutory references are to the Government Code unless
otherwise stated.

John M. Cohan
December 2, 1982
Page 2


foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect . . . on:

. . .
(c) Any source of income. . . . aggregating . . .
$250 or more in value provided to, received by or
promised to the public official within 12 months prior
to the time when the decision is made.

Before a violation of the conflict of interests provisions
of the Political Reform Act may occur, Section 87100 requires
that an official "know or have reason to know" that he or she
has a financial interest in a decision. Thus, under the
provisions of Section 87103(c), if an official actually knows
the identity of a source of income and knows that it is
reasonably foreseeable that a decision will materially affect
this source of income, the official must disqualify himself or
herself.

Sections 87100 and 87103 also require disqualification if an
official has reason to know that it is reasonably foreseeable
that a decision will materially affect a source of his or her
income. As a general rule, an official "has reason to know"
that a decision will affect a source of income whenever a
reasonable person, under the same circumstances, would be likely
to know the identity of the source of income and would be aware
of the decision's probable impact on that source. An official
engaged in a business which has numerous customers or clients is
not ordinarily required to take affirmative steps to familiarize
himself or herself with the identities of all sources of income
to the business, nor to consult his or her sources of income to
determine whether a decision will affect them.

As you can see, the question of whether an official "has
reason to know" that a decision will affect a source of income
depends on the specific facts of each situation. Thus, we can
only give you very general advice at this time. If you are
confronted, in the future, with circumstances which suggest that
an official may have to disqualify himself or herself because he
or she "has reason to know" that a decision will affect a source
of income, please feel free to contact our office. Based on the
specific facts of the situation, we will then be able to advise
the official as to whether disqualification is required under
Section 83114.

John M. Cohan
December 2, 1982
Page 3


I hope I have clarified these provisions for you. If you
have any additional questions, please feel free to contact me at
(916) 322-5901.

Very truly yours,

Janis Shank McLean
Janis Shank McLean
Counsel
Legal Division

JSM:plh

sections.question: You have requested clarification of that portion of Government Code Section 87100, which states: No public official at any level of . . . local government shall make [or] participate in the making of a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest. Specifically, you want to know how Section 87100 applies to Section 87103(c), which provides that: An official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on: (c) Any source of income, ... aggregating ... $250 or more in value provided to, received by or promised to the public official within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made.
sections.conclusion: Before a violation of the conflict of interests provisions of the Political Reform Act may occur, Section 87100 requires that an official 'know or have reason to know' that he or she has a financial interest in a decision. Thus, under the provisions of Section 87103(c), if an official actually knows the identity of a source of income and knows that it is reasonably foreseeable that a decision will materially affect this source of income, the official must disqualify himself or herself. Sections 87100 and 87103 also require disqualification if an official has reason to know that it is reasonably foreseeable that a decision will materially affect a source of his or her income. As a general rule, an official 'has reason to know' that a decision will affect a source of income whenever a reasonable person, under the same circumstances, would be likely to know the identity of the source of income and would be aware of the decision's probable impact on that source. An official engaged in a business which has numerous customers or clients is not ordinarily required to take affirmative steps to familiarize himself or herself with the identities of all sources of income to the business, nor to consult his or her sources of income to determine whether a decision will affect them.
sections.question_synthetic: How does Government Code Section 87100's conflict of interest prohibition apply to Section 87103(c)'s definition of financial interest, specifically regarding when an official must disqualify himself or herself based on sources of income?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: An official must disqualify themselves when they actually know or have reason to know that a decision will materially affect a source of their income meeting the $250 threshold. The 'reason to know' standard is an objective test based on what a reasonable person would know under the circumstances, but officials with numerous income sources are not ordinarily required to affirmatively investigate all sources unless specific facts suggest a conflict.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Standard FPPC advice letter with clear question and conclusion sections. Document dated December 2, 1982, addressed to John M. Cohan, Deputy County Counsel. Signed by Janis Shank McLean, Counsel, Legal Division.

QUESTION: You have requested clarification of that portion of Government Code Section 87100, which states: No public official at any level of . . . local government shall make [or] participate in the making of a governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest. Specifically, you want to know how Section 87100 applies to Section 87103(c), which provides that: An official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on: (c) Any source of income, ... aggregating ... $250 or more in value provided to, received by or promised to the public official within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made.

CONCLUSION: Before a violation of the conflict of interests provisions of the Political Reform Act may occur, Section 87100 requires that an official 'know or have reason to know' that he or she has a financial interest in a decision. Thus, under the provisions of Section 87103(c), if an official actually knows the identity of a source of income and knows that it is reasonably foreseeable that a decision will materially affect this source of income, the official must disqualify himself or herself. Sections 87100 and 87103 also require disqualification if an official has reason to know that it is reasonably foreseeable that a decision will materially affect a source of his or her income. As a general rule, an official 'has reason to know' that a decision will affect a source of income whenever a reasonable person, under the same circumstances, would be likely to know the identity of the source of income and would be aware of the decision's probable impact on that source. An official engaged in a business which has numerous customers or clients is not ordinarily required to take affirmative steps to familiarize himself or herself with the identities of all sources of income to the business, nor to consult his or her sources of income to determine whether a decision will affect them.

embedding.summary: FPPC Counsel responds to Santa Barbara County Counsel's inquiry regarding the application of Government Code Section 87100 (conflict of interest prohibition) to Section 87103(c) (definition of financial interest), clarifying when public officials must disqualify themselves based on the 'knows or has reason to know' standard.
